title: Chapter 43: Visit
content: Maya is thirty-two minutes late to JFK.

Not fashionably late. Not casually late. The kind of late where you leave your apartment at the exact right time and then the A train just... stops. Between stations. In a tunnel. For eleven minutes that feel like a ransom negotiation.
By the time she bursts through the arrivals door at Terminal 4, Amara Adekunle is standing beside her suitcase with the exact posture of a woman who has been counting minutes and will not mention it.
"Mom—"
"Your hair looks nice short."
Maya blinks. That's not what she expected. She expected *the traffic was* or *you know I would have* or the particular silence that means *I am not saying anything and that is the thing I am saying.*
"Thanks," Maya says. "I— the train—"
"I know." Amara picks up her suitcase. It lists to the right. Heavier on one side. Maya knows what that means before she even asks. "You look well."
"You look—" Maya almost says *tired* and catches herself a full syllable before disaster. "—great. Really great."

Amara does look great. She's wearing the indigo wrapper Maya sent for her birthday, the one from the Etsy shop in_accra_mode, and her edges are laid with a precision that suggests she did not spend nine hours on a plane so much as she spent nine hours preparing to arrive. There's a small stain on the wrapper, though. Something dark. Something that might be ogbono soup that escaped its wrapping, which means—
"Mom, did you bring food?"
Amara's expression does not change. "I brought *nourishment*."
That's a yes. That's definitely a yes. The suitcase lists further right. Maya takes the handle and nearly dislocates her shoulder.
"How much food?"
"Enough."
The car ride is a minefield of polite questions.
"How is your program?"
"Good. Really good. We just got a grant from the—"
"And your painting?"
"I'm doing a series on—"
"And your health?"
"I'm healthy, Mom."

Amara looks out the window at the Brooklyn-Queens Expressway, which is not a view that inspires confidence in anything. "Your auntie Folake had a growth removed from her neck. The size of a kola nut."
"I don't have a growth."
"You would not know. You do not go to the doctor."
"I went to the doctor. In March."

March was a lie. Maya went to the doctor in January, for a sinus infection, and the nurse asked if she wanted to update anything and she said no and that was the whole encounter. But Amara doesn't need to know that. What Amara doesn't know fills several large suitcases, all of them heavier on one side.
"Your apartment — it is clean?"
"It's... lived in."

Amara's mouth does something that is not a frown. It's the beginning of a frown that got redirected to a neutral position through sheer willpower. Maya has seen this expression approximately four thousand times in her life. It means *I will see for myself and we will both know the truth.*

They sit in traffic for twenty-three minutes. Amara asks about the neighborhood. Maya explains about the bodega on the corner, the laundromat, the woman on the third floor who plays amapiano at 7 AM every Saturday which is actually kind of nice once you get used to it. Amara listens. She does not comment on rent, safety, or the fact that Maya is living alone at twenty-seven which in Yoruba family terms means she is approximately one step from feral.
Maya does not mention Chloe.
The apartment door opens and Chloe is right there.

Not *right there* like, in the vicinity. Right there like, she has been waiting by the door and her nose is already pressing into Maya's knee before Maya has even stepped fully inside. This is unusual. Chloe usually takes a moment to assess visitors — she'll stay on the couch, or under Maya's desk, or in the warm patch by the radiator. She has opinions about new people. She takes her time.
Not today. Today Chloe is three hundred pounds of capybara muscle wedged in the entryway, sniffing at Maya's jeans, at the suitcase, at the air around Amara like she's reading a forecast.
"Mom, this is—"
Amara has stopped moving.
Not paused. Stopped. The way you stop when you see something that doesn't fit in the room you put it in. Her hand is still on the suitcase handle. Her eyes are on Chloe.
Chloe's eyes are on Amara.

Maya has seen a lot of people see Chloe for the first time. There's usually a moment — a beat — a *what the hell is that* that either becomes curiosity or fear. Amara's face does neither. Amara's face goes completely blank. Not the kind of blank that means nothing is happening. The kind of blank that means too much is happening and none of it has a category yet.
"Mom?"
Amara steps inside. She closes the door behind her. She puts down the suitcase, and it lands with the specific thud of tupperware packed with soup.
She looks at Maya.
She looks at the capybara.
She looks back at Maya.

The silence stretches for so long that Chloe sits down. Just plants her enormous bottom on the entryway floor and waits, because even Chloe apparently understands that whatever is happening requires patience.
Then Amara says something in Yoruba. Quiet. Almost to herself.
*"Baba ẹ yoo fẹẹ eleyi."*
Your father would have loved this.

Maya doesn't know if she means the capybara specifically or the chaos of it all or the fact that Maya has made a home that includes something enormous and improbable and warm. Her father died when she was fourteen. He was the one who brought home stray cats, who fed pigeons in the yard despite her mother's protests, who once spent an entire afternoon watching a snail cross the garden path and said *everything moves at its own pace, even us.* He would have loved this. Maya knows it in her sternum. But hearing her mother say it—
She doesn't cry. It's close. But she doesn't.
"Her name is Chloe," Maya says.

Amara looks at the capybara again. Her expression has shifted almost imperceptibly. The blankness has cracks in it now, and through the cracks there's something that might be wonder, or might be the beginning of acceptance, or might just be a woman from Lagos standing in a Brooklyn apartment realizing that her daughter's life is far stranger than the worst-case scenario she'd constructed on the plane.
"She is very big," Amara says.
"Yeah."
Amara unpacks food with the efficiency of a field surgeon.

Tupperware emerges from the suitcase in quantities that defy physics. Ogbono soup. Jollof rice in a container big enough to bathe a small child. Suya wrapped in foil. Puff-puff in a bag that has somehow remained warm despite a nine-hour flight and the BQE. Dried fish. Plantain. A small jar of something Maya doesn't recognize but that smells like childhood.
The fridge fills. The freezer fills. Amara starts using the shelf above the stove, which Maya didn't even know was a shelf. She thought it was a design error.
Through all of this, Amara asks what are technically questions but functionally statements. They have the rhythm of someone gathering information without admitting they need it.
"This one doesn't have a cage?"
"She's not a pet, Mom. She's— I don't know what she is. A roommate?"
"She eats fruit?"
"Mostly. Vegetables too. She loves oranges."
"How much does she cost to feed?"
It's such a mom question. So specific. So practical. So *I am calculating your financial stability based on the produce budget of a giant rodent and I will have opinions about it.*
"Less than you'd think," Maya says, which is true only if you think capybaras eat diamonds. "Dave helps. Dave's my— he's a friend. He helps with expenses."
"A friend."
"A friend."

Amara puts the last container on the shelf above the stove and closes the cabinet. She turns and looks at the apartment — really looks. The canvases against the wall, the paint-stained drop cloth, the ceiling mural that Maya spent three weeks on, the string lights that are held up by a combination of thumbtacks and optimism. The books stacked on every surface. The mug collection that has become a shelf ecosystem. The pile of blankets on the couch that is, objectively, Chloe's bed.
The giant capybara sitting in the middle of all of it, eating an orange with the slow contentment of someone who has never once questioned their place in the world.
"She made the ceiling," Maya says, pointing up at the mural — the river, the capybaras, the deep green and gold. "Well. I painted it. But it's her ceiling."
Amara looks up. Her lips part slightly. She doesn't say anything.
Chloe approaches Amara at 4:47 PM.

Maya knows the exact time because she's sitting at the kitchen table pretending to answer emails and actually watching her mother watch Chloe, which she's been doing for the past two hours with the same expression a seismologist watches a needle — waiting for the next tremor, unable to look away.

Chloe doesn't move fast. She never does when it matters. She crosses the living room floor in that slow, deliberate capybara way — not sneaking, not hesitant, just *present*. Each step placed with intention. Each breath visible in the rise and fall of her broad back.
She stops three feet from Amara's chair.
Amara has been reading a paperback — something she brought on the plane, a Nigerian thriller with a cracked spine. She doesn't look up, but Maya can see her hands have stopped turning pages.
Chloe takes another step.
Another.
Then she lowers her head and presses it against Amara's hand.
The hand that was holding the book. The hand that is now very still.

Amara freezes. Completely. The way you freeze when something unexpected touches you and you haven't decided yet whether it's a threat or a gift. Her breathing gets shallow. Her eyes go to Maya — not for help, exactly. For translation. For *what do I do with this.*
Maya doesn't move.

Chloe holds still. Her head is warm against Amara's palm. Her eyes are half-closed. She is, as far as Maya can tell, doing nothing except being present and warm and soft and exactly the right amount of weight.
Amara's hand relaxes.

It happens slowly, the way ice softens rather than cracks. Her fingers uncurl. Her palm settles against the crown of Chloe's head. Her thumb moves — almost imperceptibly at first, then with more certainty — across the short fur between Chloe's ears.
Her breathing slows.

Maya watches her mother stroke the capybara's head and thinks: this is what Chloe does. This is the thing that no one can explain and everyone can feel. She walks into a room and the room changes. Not because she tries. Because she exists, and existing quietly and completely is, apparently, enough.
Amara doesn't speak. Chloe doesn't move. The apartment holds them both.
Later — much later, after Amara has claimed the bedroom and Maya has set up the couch and Chloe has migrated to her blanket pile — Maya hears her mother's voice from behind the bedroom door.
She's on the phone. With Auntie Folake in Lagos, probably, because who else would Amara call at 10 PM on a Friday with that particular tone of controlled not-panic.

Maya shouldn't listen. She knows she shouldn't listen. She listens anyway, because she's thirty-seven percent her mother's daughter and sixty-three percent a person who lives with a giant capybara and has therefore abandoned conventional boundaries.
"There is an animal," Amara says. Her voice is low but not quiet — Amara has never mastered the concept of indoor phone volume. "In the apartment."
A pause. Auntie Folake is presumably asking what kind of animal.
"It is..." Another pause. Longer this time. Amara is searching for a word that doesn't exist. "I think it is a very large rat."
Maya presses her hand over her mouth.

"No," Amara says, more firmly. "No, it is not a rat. It is..." A third pause. The longest. When Amara speaks again, her voice is different. Softer. The way it got when her hand was on Chloe's head. "It is something else."
Maya lies back on the couch. Chloe shifts in her blanket pile and makes a sound that might be agreement or might be a dream about alfalfa.
Something else.
Yeah. That about covers it.
